_schema_notes: Read this before adding entries. Each entry describes a known interaction between two substances (supplements and/or medications). The checker matches user-entered substances against the 'a' and 'b' fields (case-insensitive, and against 'aliases') and surfaces the entry if both are present in the user's stack. DO NOT add an entry without a real, checkable source in 'sources'. DO NOT use 'severity: high' unless the source material itself characterizes it that way. Every new entry must follow this exact shape — do not invent new top-level fields without updating this notes block to explain why.

schema.a: string, primary name of substance A, lowercase
schema.b: string, primary name of substance B, lowercase
schema.aliases_a: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for A
schema.aliases_b: array of strings, alternate names/spellings for B
schema.severity: one of: 'low', 'moderate', 'high' — only use a level the source material itself supports
schema.mechanism: string, plain-language explanation of WHY this interaction happens, not just that it does
schema.summary: string, 1-3 sentences, hedge appropriately ('may', 'is associated with'), no dosage or start/stop instructions
schema.sources: array of objects: { title, url, publisher }. At least one required. Prefer NIH ODS, peer-reviewed journals, or established drug-interaction references.
schema.last_reviewed: ISO date string, set when entry is added or last verified

entries.mechanism: Warfarin works by inhibiting vitamin K-dependent clotting factors. Vitamin K intake directly opposes this mechanism, which can reduce warfarin's anticoagulant effect.
entries.summary: Vitamin K can reduce the ability of warfarin to prevent blood clotting. People on warfarin are typically advised to keep their vitamin K intake consistent rather than avoiding it entirely, since sudden changes (increases or decreases) are what destabilize INR control.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces the cytochrome P450 3A4 enzyme system, which speeds up the metabolism (breakdown) of many drugs, including warfarin, lowering their effective blood levels.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort has been associated with decreased anticoagulation effect and unstable INR values when taken with warfarin, due to faster drug metabolism.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort increases serotonin, dopamine, and norepinephrine availability through reuptake inhibition, similar to SSRIs. Combined with an SSRI, this can push serotonin levels too high.
entries.summary: Combining St. John's Wort with SSRI antidepressants may dangerously elevate serotonin levels, with a risk of serotonin syndrome, a serious condition. This combination is generally considered one to actively avoid rather than just monitor.

sources.title: The Effects of St. John's Wort and its Interactions with SSRI's

entries.mechanism: St. John's Wort induces CYP3A4 metabolism, which can lower circulating levels of the hormones in oral contraceptives.
entries.summary: St. John's Wort may reduce the effectiveness of oral contraceptives, with case reports describing breakthrough bleeding and unplanned pregnancy associated with concurrent use.

sources.title: Clinically relevant safety issues associated with St. John's wort product labels

entries.mechanism: Some chemotherapy drugs work partly by generating oxidative stress in cancer cells. High-dose antioxidant supplements could theoretically counteract this mechanism, though research on real-world clinical impact is mixed.
entries.summary: Antioxidant supplements such as vitamins C and E might reduce the effectiveness of some types of cancer chemotherapy. Anyone undergoing chemotherapy should discuss any supplement use with their oncology team before starting or continuing.

sources.title: Dietary Supplements: What You Need to Know

entries.mechanism: Iron forms insoluble complexes (chelates) with levothyroxine in the gastrointestinal tract, which reduces how much of the thyroid drug is absorbed into the bloodstream.
entries.summary: Iron supplements may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine, potentially decreasing its effectiveness in managing thyroid conditions. This interaction is noted in levothyroxine prescribing information.

entries.mechanism: Calcium binds to bisphosphonate molecules in the gastrointestinal tract, forming insoluble complexes that significantly reduce bisphosphonate absorption.
entries.summary: Calcium supplements may substantially reduce the absorption of bisphosphonate medications used for osteoporosis. This interaction is recognized in bisphosphonate prescribing guidelines.

entries.mechanism: Magnesium (along with other divalent cations like calcium, zinc, and iron) chelates fluoroquinolone antibiotics in the GI tract, forming poorly absorbed complexes and reducing the antibiotic's bioavailability.
entries.summary: Magnesium supplements may significantly reduce the absorption of fluoroquinolone antibiotics, potentially lowering their effectiveness. Antacids containing magnesium have similar effects and are explicitly noted in fluoroquinolone prescribing information.
